title: Bloodless and Wretched
subtitle: The Curse Tablet Industry of Greek Sicily, 550--100 BCE
summary: Between 550 and 100 BCE, the Greek cities of Sicily produced an extraordinary concentration of lead curse tablets — defixiones — scratched with names, folded, pierced with nails, and buried in the graves of the dead. From Kamarina's necropolis at Passo Marinaro to the workshops of Gela, from the sanctuaries of Selinus to the tombs of Messana, ordinary people paid to have their rivals, enemies, and romantic competitors condemned to be 'bloodless and wretched.' The story follows the named individuals on these tablets — Apellis the lover, Eunikos the chorus-boy, Kerdon the cursed, Chamis the sick — through a millennium of Sicilian magic.

sections.title: I. The Metal of the Dead
sections.body_html:
Why lead?

The ancient Mediterranean had many writing surfaces. Papyrus was common in Egypt, expensive elsewhere. Pottery sherds – ostraka – were used for casual writing across the Greek world. Wax tablets served for temporary notes. Stone was reserved for permanent public inscriptions. Wood was used for documents that needed to last but not forever. But for cursing, from the earliest surviving examples in the late sixth century BCE to the latest in the fifth century CE, the material of choice was almost universally lead.6

The answer lies in the properties of the metal itself. Lead is soft enough to be scratched with a bronze or iron stylus without special tools or training. It can be beaten into thin sheets with a hammer. It does not corrode easily in the acidic soils of Mediterranean cemeteries. It is cheap – a byproduct of silver mining, which was extensive in the Greek world, and available in quantity from the mines of Laurion in Attica, from Spanish and Sardinian deposits, and from local Sicilian sources.7 A scrap of lead large enough for a curse could be had for almost nothing.

But the practical qualities of lead were inseparable from its symbolic ones. Lead is cold. It is heavy. It is dull. It does not shine. The ancient Greeks associated it with death, with the underworld, with the inertia of the grave. Hesiod, in the Works and Days, uses lead as a metaphor for the weight that drags a man down.8 The Apellis tablet from Gela makes the connection explicit: “As this lead (does), so may my spells against those men draw away their honour.”9 The formula is a classic instance of what scholars call similia similibus – “like affects like” – the foundational logic of sympathetic magic. Just as the lead is cold and lifeless, so may the victims become cold and lifeless. Just as the lead is buried among the dead, so may the victims be consigned to the realm of the dead. Just as the lead is heavy and inert, so may the victims be weighed down, unable to act, unable to speak, unable to compete.10

The Greek term for these objects was katadesmos – “binding spell” – from the verb katadeo, “I bind down.”11 The Latin equivalent, defixio, from defigere, “to fix” or “to nail down,” captures a different aspect of the same ritual: the physical act of driving a nail through the folded tablet, pinning the victim’s fate as surely as the nail pinned the lead.12 Many surviving tablets bear the marks of such nailing. At least three of the Kamarina tablets were pierced.13

The ritual was simple. A person wishing to harm, constrain, or disadvantage another would inscribe the target’s name on a thin sheet of lead – sometimes with a formula, sometimes with an invocation to underworld deities, sometimes with nothing more than the name itself. The tablet would then be folded, often rolled into a small cylinder, and deposited in a place where it could reach the powers of the dead: a grave, a well, a chthonic sanctuary, a body of water connected to the underworld.14 Graves were preferred, and the graves of those who had died young or violently were considered most effective – the aoroi (those dead before their time) and the biaiothanatoi (those dead by violence), whose restless spirits were believed to linger near their remains, available for recruitment by the living.15

The depositor might perform the ritual alone, at night, in secret. Or they might employ a specialist – a goes, a ritual practitioner who knew the proper formulae and the proper gods to invoke. Whether the earliest Sicilian tablets were self-service or professionally produced is one of the central questions of the field, and we will return to it.

sections.title: II. The Island of Crossroads
sections.body_html:
Sicily was not the only place in the Greek world where curse tablets were produced. They have been found in Athens, in Boeotia, in the Peloponnese, on the islands, in the Black Sea colonies, in Magna Graecia, and eventually throughout the Roman Empire and beyond.16 But Sicily was, as far as we can tell, where the practice began – or at least where the earliest surviving examples were made.

The oldest known curse tablets in the Greek world come from Selinus (modern Selinunte) on the southwestern coast of Sicily, dating to the late sixth century or early fifth century BCE.17 Twenty-two tablets have been recovered from Selinus, most from the early fifth century, many from funerary contexts in the western necropolis and from the sanctuary area associated with Zeus Meilichios – Zeus in his chthonic, underworld aspect, the “gentle” or “appeasing” Zeus to whom the dead were commended.18 Close behind are the tablets from Gela and Kamarina, dating to the first half of the fifth century. A remarkable inscribed lead sheet from Monte Casasia, in the interior of the island, has been dated to the first half of the sixth century BCE and may be the oldest lead inscription in the Sicilian corpus – though whether it is a curse tablet, a religious text, or something else entirely remains debated.19

Why Sicily? The island’s position at the centre of the Mediterranean made it a place where Greek, Phoenician, and indigenous Sikel, Sikan, and Elymian cultures met, collided, and interpenetrated. Greek colonists had been arriving since the eighth century BCE – Syracuse was founded around 733, Gela around 689, Selinus around 628, Kamarina around 598.20 These were not isolated outposts but thriving cities with temples, theatres, agoras, and necropoleis that rivalled those of the Greek mainland. Gela was wealthy enough to attract the tragedian Aeschylus, who spent time there in the 470s and died there around 456 BCE.21 Selinus built some of the largest temples in the Greek world. Kamarina, though smaller and repeatedly destroyed and refounded, maintained a civic culture complete with phratries, magistracies, and – if the curse tablets are any guide – fiercely competitive public life.

Jessica Lamont, in her 2023 study In Blood and Ashes, has argued that the curse tablet tradition moved from west to east – from Sicily and Italy to Attica and the Aegean – contrary to the usual assumption that ritual technologies in the ancient world diffused from the eastern Mediterranean westward.22 If she is right, the Sicilian tablets are not provincial imitations of a mainland practice but the original innovation, born in the frontier conditions of the colonial west, where new communities, lacking the deep institutional structures of the old poleis, needed new mechanisms for managing conflict, competition, and anxiety.

The multicultural environment of western Sicily may have been particularly fertile for magical syncretism. The Selinus tablets were found near a sanctuary of Zeus Meilichios, a deity whose cult blended Greek and indigenous elements.23 The lead tablet from Monte Casasia contains language that some scholars have read as mixing Greek hexameter verse with local magical invocations – “subdue, and master the unwilling by force” – a phrase that straddles the boundary between religion and magic.24 The Phoenician cities of western Sicily – Motya, Panormos, Solous – had their own traditions of inscribed magic, and contact between Greek and Phoenician practitioners was inevitable in a landscape where Greek Selinus and Phoenician Motya lay barely sixty kilometres apart.

sections.title: III. The Names on the Lead
sections.body_html:
Let us look more closely at the Kamarina tablet – corpus ID 8420, designated ISic030032 – because it is one of the most revealing documents in the entire Sicilian curse corpus.

The text opens with a formula: hoi de gegrabatai epi dyspragiai – “those who follow are inscribed for failure.”25 The verb gegrabatai is the perfect passive of grapho, “I write.” The victims have been written down, enrolled on the lead, registered for ruin. The word dyspragia – “failure,” “ill-fortune,” “bad outcome” – is the opposite of eupraxia, “success.” It is a comprehensive curse: not targeted at a specific action but at the totality of the victims’ prospects.

Then come the names. Kerdon is listed first – a name meaning “the profitable one,” from kerdos, “gain” or “profit,” perhaps an artisan or trader whose very livelihood is what the curser envies.26 Puthon son of Diokles follows, the patronymic identifying him precisely within the community. Then Melanthios – “the dark-haired one” or “the one associated with dark things,” a name that appears on the Apellis tablet as well, suggesting it was common in the region, or that certain individuals attracted curses from multiple sources.27 Dion son of Parmenon. Onasimos – “the profitable” or “the useful.” Athanios. Darchon. Tellon. Euthymos – “the good-spirited.” Geloios – a name meaning either “from Gela” or “the laughable one.” Gerys. Hagnos – “the pure.” Chairis. Xenippos.

And then the closing curse: anaimatoi eston hoi de pantes dussooi – “may they be bloodless, wretched be they.”28

Anaimatoi. Without blood. The word carries a precise and chilling meaning. In Homeric Greek, blood (haima) is the substance of life itself. The dead in the underworld are anaimonoi – bloodless shades who cannot speak, cannot think, cannot act until they drink blood. When Odysseus visits the dead in Book 11 of the Odyssey, he must slaughter a ram and a ewe and let their blood flow into a trench before the ghosts can address him.29 To wish someone anaimatos is to wish them reduced to the condition of the dead while still among the living – drained of vitality, of agency, of the capacity for action. It is, in the deepest sense, a wish for a living death.

Dussooi. Wretched, unhappy, ill-fated. The word does not appear frequently in literary Greek; it belongs to the vocabulary of popular religion and magic, to the world of spoken curses and ritual imprecation rather than to the refined diction of epic or tragedy.30 Its presence here marks the tablet as a document of vernacular religion – the religion of ordinary people, not of priests or poets.

sections.title: IV. The Bones of Passo Marinaro
sections.body_html:
The cemetery where these tablets were found – Passo Marinaro – has been the subject of extensive archaeological investigation. Established southeast of the ancient walls of Kamarina, the necropolis was in use from the fifth through the third centuries BCE. Approximately 2,905 burials have been excavated in the roughly 8,000 square metres explored by archaeologists, making it one of the most thoroughly documented colonial Greek cemeteries in Sicily.31

The Italian archaeologist Federica Cordano studied the katadesmoi from Passo Marinaro and identified eleven tablets as curse texts among the much larger assemblage of lead documents recovered from the site.32 Since lead is a soft metal and Kamarina’s soil is acidic, many of the inscribed surfaces are degraded, preventing complete translation. Those for which readings are available present primarily lists of names – sometimes with phratry affiliations, sometimes with patronymics, sometimes bare names alone.

But it was not only curse tablets that were found on lead at Kamarina. The majority of the lead inscriptions from Passo Marinaro are civic documents: registration lists, probably connected to the phratry system that organized Kamarina’s citizen body. These tablets record a man’s name, his patronymic, and his phratry – for example, “Herakleidas son of Archonidas, fourth” (phratry), or “Silanos son of Ithaigeues, fourteenth,” or “Daikrates son of Lykiskos, fifth phratry.”33 Over a hundred such tablets survive, all dating to 480–450 BCE, all from the same cemetery, all on the same material.

This is an extraordinary fact. The same material – lead – served both for mundane civic record-keeping and for magical cursing. The same cemetery received both administrative documents and ritual deposits. The same hands that inscribed phratry lists may have inscribed curse tablets. The technology was identical; only the intent differed.

Why were civic records deposited in a cemetery? Several explanations have been proposed. Perhaps the records were placed in the tombs of deceased phratry members as a form of identification – a permanent register accompanying the citizen to the afterlife.34 Perhaps they were archival copies, stored in the cemetery as a secondary record in case the city’s own archives were destroyed – as happened repeatedly in Kamarina’s turbulent history. Perhaps the boundary between civic and religious life was less distinct than modern categories suggest, and the cemetery was simply a place where important documents were kept, under the protection of the dead and the gods below.

Whatever the explanation, the coexistence of administrative and magical texts at Kamarina tells us something important about the social location of curse-making. This was not an exotic, marginal practice confined to specialists and outcasts. It used the same materials, the same techniques, and in many cases the same depositional contexts as the most ordinary civic business. Cursing was embedded in the infrastructure of the polis.

sections.title: V. Apellis and the Love of Eunikos
sections.body_html:
The most remarkable curse tablet from Greek Sicily – and one of the most extraordinary documents of ancient magic anywhere in the world – is the so-called Apellis tablet, ISic030019, excavated from Gela and dated to 475–450 BCE.35

It begins with a single word: Eucha – “Prayer.”36

The word is significant. This is not presented as a curse but as a prayer, an appeal to the powers above and below. What follows is a text of astonishing complexity, combining erotic passion, competitive anxiety, sympathetic magic, and a detailed knowledge of the social world of choral competition in a mid-fifth-century Sicilian Greek city.

The full translation reads:

Prayer: Apellis, for love of Eunikos, prays that no one be taken more seriously or be more popular than Eunikos, but all should praise and admire him both willingly and unwillingly. For love of Eunikos I mark down all the khoragoi so that they be ineffectual both in word and in deed, along with their sons and fathers, and so that they fail both in the contest and outside the contests. Whoever does not leave him with me – I mark down Kaledias to keep him apart from Apellis, along with all those in between there and here. I mark down Sosias, the one from the shop of the Alkiadai, because of his love of Melanthios. Pyrias, Mysskelos, Damophantos and their khoragos I mark down, along with their sons and fathers, and all the others who arrive here. May no one be taken more seriously than Eunikos either among men or women. As this lead does, so may my spells against those men draw away their honour. For Eunikos may there be victory always, everywhere. On the lead for love of Eunikos I write.37

Let us unpack this.

Apellis is the name of the curse’s author – remarkably, he names himself, which is unusual in the defixio tradition, where anonymity was the norm. He is cursing on behalf of Eunikos, whom he loves. The word used is philotati – “for love of,” “out of affection for” – a term that in this context almost certainly carries an erotic charge.38 Apellis loves Eunikos. The love is not incidental to the curse; it is the motive, the driving force, the reason for everything that follows.

The targets of the curse are khoragoi – chorus-leaders, the men who financed and organized choral performances in civic festivals.39 In Athens, the choregos was a wealthy citizen who undertook the public liturgy of training and equipping a chorus for the dramatic and dithyrambic competitions at the Dionysia and the Lenaia. The institution existed in the Greek colonies as well, and the Apellis tablet provides our best evidence for choregic competition in fifth-century Sicily. The suggested origin of Gela makes this tablet a crucial piece of evidence, since Aeschylus worked there in the 470s, and neither he nor Pindar is likely to have been attracted to a city without a structure of poetic competitions.40

Apellis wants Eunikos to win. He wants every rival chorus-leader to fail – “both in word and in deed,” both in the contest and outside it, along with their sons and fathers. The curse is generational. It does not merely target the competitors themselves but extends to their entire families, as if the taint of failure should radiate outward through kinship networks.

Then comes a personal note. Kaledias is named specifically: Apellis wants to “keep him apart from Apellis,” suggesting that Kaledias is a rival for Eunikos’s affections. The jealousy is naked. Sosias is named next, identified with extraordinary social precision as “the one from the shop of the Alkiadai” – the establishment of the Alkiadai family, probably a commercial operation where Sosias worked or gathered.41 Sosias is cursed “because of his love of Melanthios” – that is, because Sosias loves another young man, Melanthios, and this love somehow threatens the Apellis-Eunikos bond. Perhaps Sosias is trying to draw Eunikos away from Apellis and toward Melanthios. Perhaps the erotic rivalries overlap with the competitive rivalries of the choral circuit.

And then the formula of sympathetic magic: “As this lead does, so may my spells against those men draw away their honour.” The lead is not merely a writing surface. It is an active agent in the curse, a participant in the magic. Its coldness, its weight, its deadness are transferred to the victims. Their honour – timan – is to be drawn away, drained, pulled down into the lead and into the earth.

sections.title: VI. The Same Apellis?
sections.body_html:
One of the most tantalizing questions in Sicilian epigraphy is whether the Apellis of the curse tablet is the same Apellis who appears in a commercial document from Gela, ISic030016, dated to 500–475 BCE – one generation earlier than the curse tablet.42

The commercial document is a lead letter recording a business transaction. It reads, in fragmentary form:

Myskon, son of Damis son of Kobetos […] Anthemokritos bade Apellis offer security […] said […] was; he came upon […] of the Leontines […] Myskon sitting along the avenue […] came not having the money, the price of the cattle; he did not buy […] went to Empedokles, son of Mnasimachos […] let […] become liable to act as surety.43

This is a mundane commercial text. Apellis is asked to stand as surety – a financial guarantor – in a cattle transaction. Myskon, sitting on the avenue, did not have the money. Empedokles son of Mnasimachos was consulted. The transaction apparently fell through.

The names overlap. The commercial letter mentions Apellis and Myskon. The curse tablet names Apellis and Mysskelos – the latter a variant that may or may not represent the same person.44 The dates are close: the letter dates to 500–475, the curse tablet to 475–450. If the same Apellis appears in both, then we have a man who was a known commercial figure in Gela – someone trusted enough to be asked to stand surety for a cattle deal – who also engaged in magical cursing on behalf of his beloved. The co-existence of these two documents, on the same material (lead), from the same city, within the same generation, is one of the most vivid illustrations we possess of how thoroughly magic was woven into the fabric of ordinary Greek civic and commercial life.

Peter Wilson has suggested a further connection: the commercial letter was inscribed on one side of a lead sheet, and the curse tablet on the other – an opisthographic tablet, where the lead that had already successfully guaranteed a financial transaction was recycled to guarantee a magical one.45 Just as the lead had bound Apellis’s surety to the cattle deal, so it would bind the rival chorus-leaders to failure. The magic of the document and the magic of the curse reinforced each other.

sections.title: VII. The Twenty-Two Tablets of Selinus
sections.body_html:
Selinus – Selinous in Greek, modern Selinunte – was one of the westernmost Greek cities in Sicily, founded around 628 BCE by colonists from Megara Hyblaea.46 It stood on the frontier between the Greek and Phoenician zones of influence, within sight of the Phoenician stronghold at Motya across the bay. Its position was precarious. In 409 BCE, the Carthaginian general Hannibal Mago sacked the city, killing, according to ancient sources, sixteen thousand of its inhabitants.47

Before that catastrophe, Selinus was a prosperous and ambitious city. Its temples – designated by modern scholars with letters, since their ancient dedications are mostly unknown – rank among the largest in the Greek world. Temple G, probably dedicated to Apollo, measured over 110 metres in length.48 The city’s legal and religious sophistication is attested by the famous lex sacra of Selinus, a lead tablet from the fifth century BCE that prescribes purification rituals for the community.49

The twenty-two curse tablets from Selinus are concentrated in the early fifth century BCE. Most were found in the western necropolis, some from the sand layer overlying the campo di stele, a sacred area associated with the sanctuary of Zeus Meilichios.50 Their content is predominantly judicial: the tablets target opponents in lawsuits. This is “litigation magic” – the use of curses to hamstring one’s adversary before, during, or after a legal proceeding.

The Selinus tablet designated ISic030010, corpus ID 8583, is one of the most explicit. Its target is a man named Chamis, and the curse reads: “Chamis, I hold you, and with illness’s turning-point, for failure.”51 The Greek is terse and brutal: Chamis epecho kai noso ropai ep’ ateleia. The verb epecho means “I hold fast,” “I restrain,” “I pin down.” The word nosos means “disease” or “illness.” The word ropai – “turning-point,” “crisis” – is a medical term for the critical moment in an illness when the patient either recovers or dies.52 And ateleia – “failure,” “incompletion,” “ineffectuality” – the same word that appears on the Apellis tablet when he curses the chorus-leaders to be “ineffectual.”

This curse does not merely wish failure on Chamis. It wishes him held in the crisis of his illness – pinned at the turning-point where recovery is possible but not achieved, suspended in a state of maximum suffering and minimum agency. It is a curse of extraordinary medical specificity, and it suggests that the person who composed it – whether Chamis’s enemy or a professional curse-writer working on that enemy’s behalf – possessed a detailed knowledge of how disease progresses and how to exploit its vocabulary for magical purposes.

sections.title: VIII. The Professionals and the Amateurs
sections.body_html:
Who made these tablets? The question divides scholars.

One view holds that most early Sicilian curse tablets were self-service productions. The technology was simple: anyone who could read and write could scratch names on a scrap of lead. The formulae were minimal – in many cases, nothing more than a list of names, with no invocation, no ritual language, no elaborate theology. The Kamarina name-list tablets, with their bare catalogues of targets, suggest an almost industrial simplicity: get a piece of lead, scratch the name, fold it, bury it.53

The counter-argument points to the formulaic consistency of the tablets. The Kamarina tablets use recognizably standardized language: gegrabatai epi dyspragiai (“inscribed for failure”), anaimatoi eston (“may they be bloodless”).54 The Selinus tablets employ a coherent vocabulary of binding and restraining. The Apellis tablet displays not only formulaic competence but genuine literary skill – the structure of the prayer, the deployment of the sympathetic-magic formula, the careful social identification of the targets. This level of sophistication suggests professional composition, even if the client was the one who actually scratched the letters.

The verbs of writing that appear on the tablets – engrapho (“I inscribe”), katagrapho (“I write down”), apographo (“I enrol”) – have been analysed as evidence for a distinctively literate practice of cursing that is rooted in the technology of writing itself.55 These are not the verbs of oral incantation; they are the verbs of documentation, of registration, of bureaucratic record-keeping. To curse someone by “enrolling” them on lead is to use the mechanisms of the state against them – to turn the technology of civic administration into a weapon.

Sicilian curse tablets from 500–450 BCE exhibit textual features that ground them firmly in the realm of writing rather than speech: columnar layouts, abbreviations, scribal symbols, and the kind of spatial organization found in administrative lists rather than in spontaneous composition.56 This suggests that the people who composed these texts – whether the clients themselves or hired specialists – were literate individuals familiar with the conventions of written documentation. In a society where literacy was far from universal, this is itself a significant marker of social class.

The Messana name-list defixio, ISic030330, dated to the mid-third century BCE, illustrates the simplest end of the spectrum. It lists five or six names – “Dameas son of Aristodamos, Sostratos son of Xenotimos, Sostratos son of Antaios, Aristodamos son of Sostratos” – with no curse formula, no invocation, no magical language at all.57 The names alone are the curse. To write a man’s name on lead and bury it in a tomb was, by itself, a sufficient ritual act. The lead knew what to do.

sections.title: IX. The Nymphikos Tablet: A Later Sicilian Curse
sections.body_html:
Five centuries after the Kamarina tablets, the practice of cursing on lead was still alive in Sicily – but it had changed almost beyond recognition.

The defixio from Messana designated ISic030075, corpus ID 47186, dates to the first or second century CE. It is not a simple name-list. It is a fully elaborated ritual text, invoking multiple deities by name and prescribing specific actions for the spirits of the dead.

The translation, reconstructed from the Greek, reads:

I bind down, Nymphikos, I deposit her with you, Lord of Hades, for eternal possession of her… and Kore and the Erinyes, so that from her heights she may be brought low, and you will lead her away most swiftly, and down into the earth the Erinyes shall carry the little human being. I bind down, Nymphikos, I deposit her with you, Hermes, Guide of All, so that you may lead her down into the earth most swiftly. Take from her a sacrificial offering on the thirtieth day and run, so that you may shut her up beneath the earth, and among humans no longer allow this other Medusa to go about.58

The target is a woman. She is compared to Medusa – the Gorgon whose gaze turned men to stone. The curser wants her dragged down into the earth by the Erinyes (the Furies), deposited with Hades for “eternal possession,” and shut away from the world of the living. The reference to a “sacrificial offering on the thirtieth day” – hierion triakadin – reveals that the depositor of this tablet was expected to return to the gravesite thirty days later to perform a follow-up ritual, probably an offering to the dead.59

This is no longer self-service magic. The theological complexity of the invocation – Hades, Kore (Persephone), the Erinyes, Hermes as pantagos (Guide of All) – the literary quality of the language, and the prescribed ritual timeline all point to professional composition. Someone who knew the names of the underworld powers, who knew the structure of binding spells, who knew the ritual calendar – a goes or magos, a professional practitioner of the dark arts – composed this text for a paying client.

The evolution from Kamarina to Messana – from bare name-lists to elaborate invocations, from anonymous deposits to prescribed ritual sequences, from civic cursing to personal vendetta – traces a millennium of development in the Sicilian magical tradition. The technology remained the same: lead, stylus, grave. But the content grew richer, more theologically sophisticated, more expensive, and more personal.

sections.title: X. The Geography of Cursing
sections.body_html:
If we map the known curse tablets from ancient Sicily, a pattern emerges.60

The earliest tablets – sixth century to early fifth century BCE – cluster in the Greek cities of the south and west: Selinus, Gela, Kamarina, and possibly Monte Casasia in the interior.61 These are the frontier cities, the ones closest to the Phoenician zone and to the indigenous Sikel populations of the interior. They are also the cities with the most competitive civic cultures, the ones where colonial Greek communities were still establishing themselves, jostling for position, and working out the social hierarchies that would define their civic lives.

By the fifth and fourth centuries, curse tablets appear across a wider swathe of the island. The Kamarina tablets, over a hundred strong, dominate the corpus numerically. But individual tablets or small groups have been found at other sites across Sicily. Lilybaeum (modern Marsala), on the western tip of the island, produced two curse tablets that provide the only Sicilian examples of the verb katadeo – “I bind down” – the classical term for curse-making.62 Morgantina, an inland site with both Greek and indigenous occupation, yielded ten curse tablets from a pit in the sanctuary of the Chthonic Deities, dating to around 100 BCE. Four of these tablets target the same woman – a slave named Venusta – using nearly identical wording, suggesting either a particularly determined client or a professional who produced multiple tablets for the same commission.63

The geographic spread of the practice suggests that cursing was not confined to a single city or a single cultural tradition but was a pan-Sicilian phenomenon, shared across the Greek communities of the island and potentially adopted by – or shared with – non-Greek populations as well.

sections.title: XI. The Phratry Tablets: A City Written in Lead
sections.body_html:
We must not lose sight of the non-magical lead tablets from Kamarina, because they provide the social context without which the curse tablets cannot be fully understood.

Between 480 and 450 BCE, the citizens of Kamarina produced a remarkable series of lead documents recording the names of individuals, their fathers’ names, and their phratry affiliations.64 More than 130 of these tablets survive in the corpus, each typically recording a single name: “Aischylos son of Aristios, fourteenth phratry.” “Antisthenes son of Parmenon, fourteenth phratry.” “Herakleidas son of Archonidas, fourth phratry.” “Silanos son of Ithaigeues, fourteenth phratry.” “Daikrates son of Lykiskos, fifth phratry.”65

The phratries – kinship-based subdivisions of the citizen body – were fundamental to the political and social organization of Greek cities. At Kamarina, the tablets attest at least fourteen named phratries, each identified by an ordinal number: first, fourth, fifth, eighth, ninth, eleventh, twelfth, fourteenth.66 Some tablets add financial notations: dekalitron – “ten litrai” – perhaps a registration fee, a tax assessment, or a record of property qualification.67

The names on the phratry tablets overlap with the names on the curse tablets. Melanthios appears on both the Kamarina curse tablet (8420) and the Apellis tablet from Gela (8373). Sosias appears on both the Apellis tablet and the Kamarina name-list defixio ISic030031, which lists “Sosias son of Archonidas” and “Sosias son of Archias” among a catalogue of targets including Menon, Echecratides, Archedamos, Aristodamos, Charipos, Archethales, Chiron, Herakleidas, and Archytas.68 The name Geloios – “the Geloan” or “the one from Gela” – appears on the curse tablet and perhaps betrays the target’s origin in the neighbouring city. Some of these are common names; the overlap may be coincidental. But in a small colonial community where a few hundred families constituted the entire citizen body, the probability of genuine connections is high.

The phratry tablets reveal a society organized into competitive corporate groups. Each phratry had its own identity, its own internal hierarchy, and presumably its own rivalries with other phratries. The curse tablets, many of which target multiple individuals, may reflect inter-phratry competition – one faction cursing the members of a rival faction in advance of an election, a festival, or a legal proceeding.

sections.title: XII. The Monte Casasia Enigma
sections.body_html:
The oldest lead inscription from Sicily – and possibly the oldest lead tablet in the Greek world – was found at Monte Casasia, an inland site in the territory between Kamarina and Syracuse. It has been dated to the first half of the sixth century BCE, making it roughly contemporary with the earliest Greek curse tablets from anywhere.69

The text is fragmentary and heavily debated. What survives reads, in translation:

[…] from the garden, driving […] by name Tetragos […] windswept shore […] of waters […] blessed is he who […] these things throughout his house and in his mind holds […] of the blessed ones along the wagon-road […] the voice […] subdue, and master the unwilling by force […].70

The language is remarkable. “Blessed is he who” (olbios ho) is a formula associated with mystery religions – particularly the Orphic and Eleusinian traditions, where initiates were promised blessedness in the afterlife.71 “Subdue, and master the unwilling by force” (damnameneu damason de kakos aekontas anankiai) is a command formula that belongs squarely to the world of magic – the language of compelling, of forcing, of overcoming resistance through supernatural power.72

Is this a curse tablet? A religious text? A magical hymn? An Orphic gold leaf inscribed on lead rather than gold? The debate continues. What is clear is that by the early sixth century BCE, someone in the interior of Sicily was inscribing Greek text on lead, using language that blurred the boundaries between religion, magic, and poetry. The technology and the vocabulary were already in place for the explosion of curse-tablet production that would follow in the next century.

sections.title: XIII. The Hybla Fragment: The End of the Tradition
sections.body_html:
At the other end of the timeline stands a fragment from Hybla Heraea (modern Ragusa), in the southeastern corner of the island, dated to the fourth or fifth century CE – nearly a millennium after the Kamarina tablets.73

The text is brief and damaged:

[…] come out, devil! Adonai […] Sabaoth […] ARAKIA […].74

Exelthe diabole – “come out, devil” – is an exorcism formula. Adonai and Sabaoth are Jewish divine names, widely adopted in the syncretic magical tradition of late antiquity. The combination of Jewish divine names with a Greek exorcism command is typical of the late antique magical koine – a shared vocabulary of power-words drawn from multiple religious traditions and deployed by practitioners who served clients regardless of their faith.75

This fragment from Hybla tells us that lead continued to serve as a medium for magical inscription in Sicily for nearly a thousand years after the earliest tablets at Selinus and Monte Casasia. The practitioners had changed. The gods had changed. The language had shifted from classical Greek to the syncretic idiom of late antiquity, mixing Hebrew, Greek, and unintelligible voces magicae. But the material – lead – and the purpose – to command supernatural forces through inscribed words – remained constant. The curse tablet industry of Greek Sicily was not a brief phenomenon but a deep and enduring tradition, rooted in the island’s soil as stubbornly as the lead itself.

sections.title: XIV. The Social World of the Curse
sections.body_html:
Who commissioned these tablets? For whom were they made? And against whom were they directed?

The surviving evidence suggests several broad categories of motive.76

Competition. The Apellis tablet targets rivals in choregic competition – an agonistic context where prestige, patronage, and public honour were at stake. Competitive cursing is well attested in Athens, where curse tablets have been found targeting rival craftsmen, rival litigants, rival athletes, and rival performers.77 In Sicily, the colonial context may have intensified the competitive pressures. In a newly founded city, social hierarchies were not inherited from ancient tradition but actively constructed through public performance, wealth display, and institutional achievement. The stakes of a choral competition in fifth-century Gela were not merely artistic; they were existential. To win was to establish your faction, your patron, your beloved as dominant. To lose was to concede that dominance to someone else.

Litigation. The Selinus tablets target opponents in lawsuits. This is the most common category of Greek curse tablets across the entire Mediterranean. A litigant who feared losing his case might commission a curse to silence the opposing speaker, confuse the opposing witnesses, or sway the judges.78 The Chamis tablet – “I hold you in the crisis of illness so that you cannot achieve your objectives” – may be a litigation curse disguised as a medical curse, aimed at preventing Chamis from appearing in court or performing effectively if he did.

Erotic rivalry. Apellis curses Kaledias to keep him away from Eunikos. Sosias is cursed because of his love for Melanthios. The erotic dimension of the Apellis tablet is inseparable from the competitive dimension: love, patronage, and public performance were intertwined in a world where a wealthy man’s attachment to a young performer was both a personal passion and a civic investment.

Commerce. The presence of the Apellis commercial letter – recording a cattle transaction involving surety and payment disputes – alongside the curse tablet suggests that commercial rivalries could also motivate cursing. If your business competitor was thriving and you were not, a curse tablet offered a way to level the field that did not require you to improve your own products.

Personal hatred. The Messana tablet against the unnamed woman compared to Medusa is pure malice. The curser wants the target dragged to the underworld by the Furies, shut beneath the earth, removed from the world of the living. The language of this tablet is not competitive or strategic; it is visceral. Someone hated this woman with a hatred that demanded supernatural intervention.

sections.title: XV. The Technology of Despair
sections.body_html:
There is something deeply human in these objects. A man sits in the dark, near a grave, scratching names on a thin sheet of lead. He folds the sheet. He drives a nail through it. He pushes it into the earth, into the gap between the stones of a burial, or drops it down a well, or leaves it at the threshold of a chthonic sanctuary. Then he walks away, back to the city, back to the agora, back to the competition or the courtroom or the shop, carrying with him the knowledge that somewhere beneath the ground, a piece of lead bears the names of his enemies, and the dead know those names.

Did it work? The question is not answerable in the terms the ancients would have understood. The curse tablets do not come with follow-up reports. We do not know whether Kerdon failed, whether Chamis remained sick, whether Eunikos won his competition, whether the woman from Messana was brought low. What we know is that people kept making these tablets for over a thousand years – from the sixth century BCE to the fifth century CE and beyond – across the entire Mediterranean world, in Greek, Latin, Aramaic, Hebrew, Coptic, and demotic Egyptian.79 The practice was not marginal. It was not confined to the ignorant or the desperate. It was a technology of spiritual agency, available to anyone who could afford a scrap of lead and a visit to the cemetery.

The more than 1,500 curse tablets that have been recovered from the ancient Mediterranean world represent, as John Gager has argued, a dimension of classical life that was previously inaccessible – the world of private religion, personal anxiety, and individual agency in the face of uncontrollable circumstances.80 The official religion of the Greek polis – its temples, its festivals, its public sacrifices – addressed the community as a whole. The curse tablets addressed the individual. They were private rituals for private problems: the rival who was too successful, the lover who was straying, the opponent who might win the case, the enemy who would not die.

In Greek Sicily, where colonial communities were building new cities on contested ground, where Greek met Phoenician met Sikel in a landscape of competing cultures and overlapping divinities, where competition for status was fierce and the institutional mechanisms for managing conflict were still being worked out – in that world, the curse tablet was not a marginal curiosity. It was a social technology, as essential in its way as the assembly, the law court, or the marketplace. It was the weapon of the powerless against the powerful, the anxious against the confident, the lover against the rival. And it was written on lead, because lead is cold, and heavy, and lasts forever, like hatred, like desire, like the dead.

Anaimatoi eston hoi de pantes dussooi.

May all of them be bloodless, wretched be they.

Somewhere beneath the soil of Kamarina, the lead remembers.

notes.text: ISic030032 (corpus ID 8420), Kamarina, 460--440 BCE. The Greek closing formula reads: *anaimatoi eston hoi de pantes dussooi*. Translation based on Dubois's French: "Que tous ceux-ci soient exsangues, malheureux soient-ils." The present translation renders the Greek more literally.

ISic030032 (corpus ID 8420). The tablet opens: *hoi de gegrabatai epi dyspragiai* -- "those who follow are inscribed for failure." On the typical dimensions and materiality of Greek curse tablets, see D.R. Jordan, "A Survey of Greek Defixiones Not Included in the Special Corpora," *Greek, Roman, and Byzantine Studies* 26 (1985): 151--197.

ISic030032 (corpus ID 8420). The names are read from the Greek text: Kerdon, Puthon son of Diokles, Melanthios, Dion son of Parmenon, Onasimos, Athanios, Darchon, Tellon, Euthymos, Geloios, Gerys, Hagnos, Chairis, Xenippos. Some readings are uncertain due to damage.

On the necropolis of Passo Marinaro, southeast of Kamarina, and the archaeological context of the lead tablets, see G. Pelagatti, "Camarina: relazione preliminare della campagna di scavi 1961--1965," *Bollettino d'Arte* 51 (1966). On the piercing of tablets, see J.G. Gager, *Curse Tablets and Binding Spells from the Ancient World* (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1992), 18--21.

F. Cordano identified eleven katadesmoi among the lead assemblage from Passo Marinaro. See F. Cordano, "Le tessere pubbliche del tempio di Atena a Camarina," *Quaderni di Archeologia della Libia* 21 (2006), and the analysis in the research file at corpus ID 8420.

On the overwhelming preference for lead as the medium for curse tablets across the Greek and Roman worlds, see Gager, *Curse Tablets*, 3--7; Jordan, "Survey of Greek Defixiones," 151--155; and J.L. Lamont, *In Blood and Ashes: Curse Tablets and Binding Spells in Ancient Greece* (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2023), ch. 1.

On lead as a byproduct of silver mining, see R.J. Forbes, *Studies in Ancient Technology*, vol. 8 (Leiden: Brill, 1971), 193--220. The Laurion mines in Attica were the principal Greek source; Sicilian and Sardinian deposits also contributed.

Hesiod, *Works and Days*, line 741: lead appears as a symbol of heaviness. On the symbolic properties of lead in magical thought, see the discussion in C. Faraone, "The Agonistic Context of Early Greek Binding Spells," in C. Faraone and D. Obbink, eds., *Magika Hiera: Ancient Greek Magic and Religion* (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1991), 3--32.

ISic030019 (corpus ID 8373), Gela, 475--450 BCE: "As this lead (does), so may my spells against those men draw away their honour" -- *hos houtos ho bolimos tos te...epoidi ai timan erysainto*. Translation by P. Wilson and F. Favi.

On the *similia similibus* formula in curse tablets, see J. Franek and D. Urbanová, "'May Their Limbs Melt, Just as This Lead Shall Melt...': Sympathetic Magic and Similia Similibus Formulae in Greek and Latin Curse Tablets (Part 1)," *Philologia Classica* 14.1 (2019): 27--55. The analogy is foundational to the theory of sympathetic magic as formulated by J.G. Frazer, *The Golden Bough* (1890), and refined by subsequent scholarship.
The Greek term *katadesmos* (κατάδεσμος) derives from *kata* ("down") + *deo* ("I bind"). See Gager, *Curse Tablets*, 3.
The Latin term *defixio* derives from *defigere*, "to fix down, to nail." See A. Audollent, *Defixionum Tabellae* (Paris, 1904), the foundational corpus.

At least three Kamarina tablets bear nail-holes. See the discussion in Cordano's analysis and the archaeological reports from Passo Marinaro. On the ritual significance of nailing, see Gager, *Curse Tablets*, 18--21.
On the depositional contexts of curse tablets -- graves, wells, chthonic sanctuaries, bodies of water -- see Lamont, *In Blood and Ashes*, ch. 2; Gager, *Curse Tablets*, 18--22.

On the *aoroi* (premature dead) and *biaiothanatoi* (violently dead) as preferred intermediaries for curse magic, see S.I. Johnston, *Restless Dead: Encounters Between the Living and the Dead in Ancient Greece* (Berkeley: University of California Press, 1999), chs. 3--4.

Jordan, "Survey of Greek Defixiones," catalogues examples from across the Greek world. Jordan's 2000 supplement, "New Greek Curse Tablets (1985--2000)," *Greek, Roman, and Byzantine Studies* 41 (2000): 5--46, adds approximately one hundred more.

On the Selinus tablets as among the earliest surviving defixiones, see Lamont, *In Blood and Ashes*, ch. 3; M. Bettarini, *Corpus delle defixiones di Selinunte* (Alessandria: Edizioni dell'Orso, 2005). Also: L. Ferreri, "The Selinunte Defixiones as Evidence for Epigraphic Literacy and Western Sicilian Identities," in *Sicilian Identities* (2019).

On the sanctuary of Zeus Meilichios at Selinus and the deposition of curse tablets in the *campo di stele*, see V. Hinz, *Der Kult von Demeter und Kore auf Sizilien und in der Magna Graecia* (Wiesbaden: Reichert, 1998); and C. Grotta, *Zeus Meilichios a Selinunte* (Rome, 2010).

ISic030317 (corpus ID 71), Monte Casasia, first half of the 6th century BCE. The text is fragmentary and its classification remains debated. See R. Arena, *Iscrizioni greche arcaiche di Sicilia e Magna Grecia*, vol. 2 (Milan, 1996).

On the foundation dates and histories of the major Greek colonies in Sicily, see T.J. Dunbabin, *The Western Greeks* (Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1948); I. Malkin, *Religion and Colonization in Ancient Greece* (Leiden: Brill, 1987).

On Aeschylus in Gela, see the *Vita Aeschyli* and Plutarch, *Cimon* 8.8. Pausanias 1.14.5 records that Aeschylus died at Gela. Peter Wilson has argued that the Apellis tablet is evidence for choregic competition at Gela in the 470s--450s; see P. Wilson, "Sicilian Choruses," in P. Wilson, ed., *The Greek Theatre and Festivals* (Oxford: Oxford University Press, 2007).

Lamont, *In Blood and Ashes*, introduction and ch. 1: "a continuous history of curse-tablets from archaic to late Hellenistic times, beginning in Sicily and Italy then moving eastward to Attica, the Aegean, and beyond -- contrary to modern expectations that such ritual technologies generally moved from East to West."
On the cult of Zeus Meilichios at Selinus and its possible blend of Greek and indigenous elements, see Grotta, *Zeus Meilichios a Selinunte* (2010).

ISic030317 (corpus ID 71): "subdue, and master the unwilling by force" -- *damnameneu damason de kakos aekontas anankiai*. The imperative *damason* ("subdue!") and the noun *anankia* ("force," "necessity") belong to the vocabulary of magical compulsion.
ISic030032 (corpus ID 8420): *hoi de gegrabatai epi dyspragiai*.
The name *Kerdon* derives from *kerdos* ("gain," "profit"). It is attested as a common name among artisans and traders; see LGPN (Lexicon of Greek Personal Names).

*Melanthios* appears on ISic030032 (corpus ID 8420) at Kamarina and on ISic030019 (corpus ID 8373) at Gela, where Sosias is cursed "because of his love of Melanthios." Whether the same individual is meant cannot be determined, but the name is not extremely common in the Sicilian corpus.
ISic030032 (corpus ID 8420): *anaimatoi eston hoi de pantes dussooi*.

Homer, *Odyssey* 11.34--43: Odysseus digs a trench and pours libations, then sacrifices animals so that the dead may drink blood and speak. The *anaimonoi nekroi* -- bloodless dead -- are a fundamental concept in Greek eschatology.

*Dussooi* (δύσσοοι) -- "wretched," "ill-fated" -- is rare in literary Greek. Its appearance on the Kamarina tablet marks it as belonging to the register of popular, ritual, or vernacular language rather than to the literary koine.

On the Passo Marinaro necropolis at Kamarina, see Pelagatti, "Camarina: relazione preliminare" (1966); and C.W. Weiss, *The Bioarchaeology of Classical Kamarina: Life and Death in Greek Sicily* (Gainesville: University Press of Florida, 2017), reviewed in *American Journal of Archaeology* 121.3 (2017).
Cordano's study of the Kamarina katadesmoi is discussed in the research analysis accompanying corpus ID 8420. See also the I.Sicily entries for ISic030028 through ISic030260.

Examples from the Kamarina phratry tablets: ISic030122 (corpus ID 8423): "Herakleidas son of Archonidas, fourth"; ISic030124 (corpus ID 8425): "Silanos son of Ithaigeues, fourteenth"; ISic030125 (corpus ID 8426): "Daikrates son of Lykiskos, fifth phratry"; ISic030029 (corpus ID 8417): "Aischylos son of Aristios, fourteenth."

On the possible functions of lead phratry tablets deposited in the Kamarina necropolis, see Cordano's analysis and the discussion in L. Dubois, *Inscriptions grecques dialectales de Sicile* (Rome: Ecole francaise de Rome, 1989), no. 120--135.

ISic030019 (corpus ID 8373), Gela, 475--450 BCE. The tablet is opisthographic (inscribed on both sides); the curse occupies one face, a commercial document the other. Translation by P. Wilson and F. Favi.
ISic030019 (corpus ID 8373): *Eucha* (Εὐχά) -- "Prayer" or "Vow." The word positions the curse as a religious act, an appeal to divine power.
ISic030019 (corpus ID 8373), full translation. See the corpus entry and Wilson's analysis in *The Greek Theatre and Festivals*.

The word *philotati* (φιλότατι) in the Apellis tablet carries strong erotic connotations. In Homeric and archaic Greek, *philotes* can mean "love," "friendship," or specifically "sexual love." The repeated phrase *epi philotati tai Eunikou* -- "for love of Eunikos" -- frames the entire curse as an act of erotic devotion.

On the *choregos* (χορηγός) and the choregic system, see P. Wilson, *The Athenian Institution of the Khoregia* (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2000). The Apellis tablet uses the Doric form *khoragos* (χοραγός).

Wilson, in *The Greek Theatre and Festivals*, argues that the Apellis tablet provides crucial evidence for organized choregic competition at Gela, and that the presence of such competition helps explain the city's attraction for Aeschylus and Pindar.

ISic030019 (corpus ID 8373): *Sosian apographo apo tou kapeleiou Alkidan* -- "I mark down Sosias, the one from the shop of the Alkiadai." *Kapeleion* is a "shop" or "tavern"; the Alkiadai are a family whose establishment serves as a social identifier.
ISic030016 (corpus ID 8389), Gela, 500--475 BCE. A lead letter recording a commercial transaction involving surety and cattle.

ISic030016 (corpus ID 8389), translation by Miller: "Myskon, son of Damis son of Kobetos [...] Anthemokritos bade Apellis offer security [...] Myskon sitting along the avenue [...] came not having the money, the price of the cattle; he did not buy [...] went to Empedokles, son of Mnasimachos."

The name *Mysskelos* on the curse tablet (ISic030019, corpus ID 8373) and *Myskon* on the commercial letter (ISic030016, corpus ID 8389) are different names but share the root *Mysk-/Myssk-*. The identification is speculative.

Wilson's interpretation of the opisthographic nature of the Apellis tablet -- that the successful commercial use of the lead reinforced its magical efficacy -- is discussed in *The Greek Theatre and Festivals* and in the Livius.org commentary on the Sicilian curse tablet.
On the foundation and history of Selinus, see Dunbabin, *The Western Greeks*, ch. 6; M.H. Jameson, D.R. Jordan, and R.D. Kotansky, *A Lex Sacra from Selinous* (Durham: Duke University, 1993).
Diodorus Siculus 13.57 records the sack of Selinus in 409 BCE by the Carthaginian army under Hannibal Mago. The death toll of sixteen thousand is given by Diodorus.
On the temples of Selinus, see D. Mertens, *Selinus I: Die Stadt und ihre Mauern* (Mainz: von Zabern, 2003). Temple G measured approximately 110.36 metres in length.

The *lex sacra* of Selinus: Jameson, Jordan, and Kotansky, *A Lex Sacra from Selinous* (1993). This lead tablet prescribes purification rituals and is one of the most important documents of Greek sacred law from the colonial west.
On the findspots of the Selinus defixiones, see Bettarini, *Corpus delle defixiones di Selinunte*; and Ferreri, "The Selinunte Defixiones as Evidence for Epigraphic Literacy."

ISic030010 (corpus ID 8583), Selinus, 499--450 BCE: *Chamis epecho kai noso ropai ep' ateleia*. Translation based on Dubois's French: "Je te maintiens dans la crise fatale de la maladie afin que tu ne puisses atteindre tes objectifs."

The medical term *rhope* (ῥοπή) -- "turning-point," "crisis" -- appears in the Hippocratic corpus to describe the critical moment in an illness. See W.H.S. Jones, ed., *Hippocrates*, vol. 1 (Loeb Classical Library, 1923), *Prognostic* 1.
The "self-service" model for early Sicilian curse tablets is argued by C. Faraone, "The Agonistic Context of Early Greek Binding Spells," in Faraone and Obbink, *Magika Hiera* (1991), 3--32.
The formulaic consistency of the Kamarina tablets is noted by Dubois, *Inscriptions grecques dialectales de Sicile*, and by Cordano in her analysis of the Passo Marinaro corpus.

On the verbs of writing in Sicilian curse tablets -- *engrapho*, *katagrapho*, *apographo* -- and their implications for the literate character of the practice, see Ferreri, "The Selinunte Defixiones as Evidence for Epigraphic Literacy," and Lamont, *In Blood and Ashes*, ch. 3.

On the textual features of Sicilian curse tablets -- columnar layouts, abbreviations, scribal symbols -- see Ferreri, "The Selinunte Defixiones," and Bettarini, *Corpus delle defixiones di Selinunte*.
ISic030330 (corpus ID 12734), Messana, mid-3rd century BCE: "Dameas son of Aristodamos, Sostratos son of Xenotimos, Sostratos son of Antaios, Aristodamos son of Sostratos, Aristodamos."

ISic030075 (corpus ID 47186), Messana, 1st--2nd century CE. Translation reconstructed from the Greek text. On the theological apparatus of this tablet, see the research analysis accompanying corpus ID 47186.

ISic030075 (corpus ID 47186): *hierion triakadin* -- "sacrificial offering on the thirtieth day." The prescribed follow-up ritual suggests a structured magical practice requiring multiple visits to the depositional site.

On the geographic distribution of Sicilian curse tablets, see the comprehensive survey in A. Castiglione, "A New Sicilian Curse Corpus: A Blueprint for a Geographical and Chronological Analysis of Defixiones from Sicily" (2019).

The earliest Sicilian tablets cluster at Selinus (late 6th--early 5th c. BCE), Gela (500--450 BCE), Kamarina (480--440 BCE), and Monte Casasia (first half of 6th c. BCE). See Bettarini, Dubois, Arena, and Cordano for the respective corpora.

On the two curse tablets from Lilybaeum, see D.R. Jordan, "Two Curse Tablets from Lilybaeum," *Greek, Roman, and Byzantine Studies* 38 (1997): 387--396. These provide the only Sicilian instances of the verb *katadeo*.

On the Morgantina curse tablets, see the publication in *Axon* 5.2 (2021): "A Greek Defixio from Morgantina." Four of ten tablets from the pit in the sanctuary of the Chthonic Deities target a slave named Venusta using nearly identical wording.
The Kamarina phratry tablets (ISic030028 through ISic030260 and beyond) number over 130 in the corpus, all dating to 480--450 BCE. See Cordano's publications and the I.Sicily entries.

ISic030029 (corpus ID 8417): "Aischylos son of Aristios, fourteenth"; ISic030030 (corpus ID 8418): "Antisthenes son of Parmenon, fourteenth phratry"; ISic030122 (corpus ID 8423): "Herakleidas son of Archonidas, fourth"; ISic030124 (corpus ID 8425): "Silanos son of Ithaigeues, fourteenth"; ISic030125 (corpus ID 8426): "Daikrates son of Lykiskos, fifth phratry."

Phratry numbers attested on the Kamarina tablets include: first (*prata*), fourth (*tetarta*), fifth (*pemptas*), eighth (*ogdoa*), ninth (*henatas*), eleventh (*hendekatas*), twelfth (*duodekatas*), and fourteenth (*tetartas epi deka*). See the corpus entries ISic030122--ISic030260.
The financial notation *dekalitron* ("ten litrai") appears on several Kamarina tablets, e.g., ISic030131 (corpus ID 8432): "Pyrrichos son of Nikophanes, dekalitron, eighth."

ISic030031 (corpus ID 8419): name-list including "Menon son of Dameas, Echecratides, Archedamos, Aristodamos, Charipos, Archethales son of Chiron, Herakleidas, Archytas, Sosias son of Archonidas, Sosias son of Archias, Symarias, Sikanas, Timokrateia, Polemainetos."
ISic030317 (corpus ID 71), Monte Casasia, first half of the 6th century BCE. On the dating, see Arena, *Iscrizioni greche arcaiche di Sicilia e Magna Grecia*, vol. 2.
ISic030317 (corpus ID 71), translation. The fragmentary text combines elements of hexameter verse, mystery-religion language (*olbios*), and magical compulsion (*damason*).

The formula *olbios hos* -- "blessed is he who" -- appears in the Orphic gold leaves from Thurii and elsewhere in southern Italy. See F. Graf and S.I. Johnston, *Ritual Texts for the Afterlife: Orpheus and the Bacchic Gold Tablets* (London: Routledge, 2007).

ISic030317 (corpus ID 71): *damnameneu damason de kakos aekontas anankiai* -- "subdue, and master the unwilling by force." The word *ananke* ("necessity," "compulsion") is a key term in Greek magical vocabulary.
ISic002761 (corpus ID 24144), Hybla Heraea, 4th--5th century CE.
ISic002761 (corpus ID 24144): *exelthe diabole Adonai...Sabaoth...ARAKIA*. The formula *exelthe* ("come out!") is standard in late antique exorcism texts.

On the syncretic use of Jewish divine names (*Adonai*, *Sabaoth*, *Iao*) in late antique magical texts, see Gager, *Curse Tablets*, ch. 1; and G. Bohak, *Ancient Jewish Magic: A History* (Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2008).

The categories of motive behind curse tablets -- competition, litigation, erotics, commerce -- are systematically analysed in Gager, *Curse Tablets*, chs. 3--7, and in Faraone, "The Agonistic Context of Early Greek Binding Spells."

On competitive cursing in Athens -- targeting craftsmen, litigants, athletes, and performers -- see Lamont, *In Blood and Ashes*, chs. 4--6; and W. Riess, T. Gellar-Goad, and J.L. Lamont, *Magic in Ancient Athens: A Complete Translation of Attic Curse Tablets* (forthcoming).
On litigation curses, see Gager, *Curse Tablets*, ch. 4: "Tongue-Tied in Court: Curses Against Litigation." The Selinus tablets are the earliest examples of this category.

On the chronological and geographic range of the curse tablet tradition -- from the 6th century BCE to the 5th century CE and beyond, across the entire Mediterranean -- see Jordan, "Survey of Greek Defixiones" (1985) and "New Greek Curse Tablets" (2000); Gager, *Curse Tablets*; and Lamont, *In Blood and Ashes*.

Gager, *Curse Tablets and Binding Spells from the Ancient World* (1992), introduction: these texts "shed light on a new dimension of classical study previously inaccessible," extending "into every aspect of ancient life -- athletic and theatrical competitions, judicial proceedings, love affairs, business rivalries, and the recovery of stolen property."

sources.description: Apellis love-competition curse tablet, Gela

sources.translation: Prayer: Apellis, for love of Eunikos (prays) that no one be taken more seriously or be more popular than Eunikos, but (all) should praise and admire (him) both willingly and unwillingly. For Love of Eunikos I mark down all the khoragoi so that they be ineffectual both in word and in deed, along with their sons and fathers, and so that they fail both in the contest and outside the contests. Whoever does not leave him (sc. Eunikos) with me. I mark down Kaledias to keep him apart from Apellis, along with all those in between there and here [fortasse 'all those there against the arbiters here']. I mark down Sosias, the one from the shop of the Alkiadai, bacause of his love of (Mel)anthios. Pyrias, Mysskelos, Damophantos and their khoragos I mark down, along with their sons and fathers, and all the others who arrive here. May no one be taken more seriously than Eunikos either among men or women. As this lead (does), so may my spells against those men draw away their honour. For Eunikos may there be victory always, everywhere. on the lead for love of Eunikos I write [Trans. P. Wilson F. Favi]

[...] Myskon, son of Damis son of Kobetos [...] Anthemokritos bade Apellis offer security 
                    [...] said [...] was; he came upon [...] of the Leontines [...] Myskon sitting along the avenue [...] 
                    came (not?) having the money, the price of the cattle; he did not buy [...] went to Empedokles, 
                    son of Mnasimachos [...] let [...] become (liable?) to act as surety (trans. by Miller).
